answer:
In Mirza Ghalib's ghazal "Tum apne shikwe ki baatein na khod khod ke poocho," the word *wisaal* (union/intimacy) evokes an unambiguously erotic intensification of desire, likened to the froth on a wine cup that only heightens thirst—complete with imagery of lips (*lab*), goblet (*qadah*), and greedy longing (*shauq-e-dil-e-harees*):[^1]

> The greedy heart's desire grew more intense with intimacy;  
> The foam upon the wine-cup's lip is thirst's own ecstasy.[^1]

In Allama Iqbal's "Do Sitare" ("Two Stars") from *Bang-e-Dara Part 3*, the same word *wisaal* describes stars' longing for perpetual union (*wisaal ki tamanna*), but this cosmic yearning is thwarted by the inexorable laws of motion and fate (*gardish taaron ka hai muqaddar*, "the revolution of stars is fated"), redirecting any latent eroticism toward a metaphysical meditation on transience and divine decree:[^2]

> A dream of lasting union's plea,  
> For parting is the cosmos's decree.[^2]

Yes, *wisaal* is the identical Urdu word (وصال) in both, drawn directly from the original texts via transliteration.[^3][^4]

[^1]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch.193 ("Tum apne shikwe ki baatein na khod khod ke poocho"), [paragraph](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/193-tum-apne-shikwe-ki-baatein-na-khod-khod-ke-poocho/translation/#p-249463).
[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch.5 ("Do Sitare"), [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/05-do-sitare/translation/#p-b52cf6).
[^3]: Ghalib ch.193, transliteration: "Hua wisaal se shauq-e-dil-e-harees ziyaada" ([chapter](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/193-tum-apne-shikwe-ki-baatein-na-khod-khod-ke-poocho/transliteration/)).
[^4]: Iqbal ch.5, transliteration: "Lekin yeh wisaal ki tamanna" ([chapter](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/05-do-sitare/transliteration/)).
